Swiss watch exports fell for a third straight month in November due to a drop in shipments to China, while prices for the most sought-after models fell to a three-year low in 2024.

Vontobel analyst Jean-Philippe Bertschy flagged pressure from Swatch’s exposure to entry-level products, as well as its high inventory levels. Swatch’s performance this year has even lagged behind Kering SA , which is suffering its own problems in trying to turn around its Gucci brand.

Luxury labels that cater to the ultra-wealthy, such as France’s Hermes International or Brunello Cucinelli SpA , have been more resilient given their clients are insulated from swings in the economy. That’s been seen in recent earnings reports from the Italian fashion house as well as by Switzerland’s Richemont, which owns the Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels jewelry brands.

Finally, both Vontobel’s Bertschy and Kepler Cheuvreux’s Cox note tension between the company and the financial community. Swatch has hit back at investors who have called out the firm for its governance and management practices after it was questioned on its lack of shareholder engagement.

A spokesperson for Swatch declined to comment.

The nearly two-year decline in Swatch’s shares did see some respite last September amid buyout rumors .

Chief Executive Office Nick Hayek said then it was “pure speculation” that the company is considering going private now, although it was something that “would be nice to do.”

item.title: Logan Said Creditors Holding 66% Debt Agree to Overhaul Plan
item.description:
• Another 12% of creditors have indicated support, Logan said
  • Builder extends early-bird deadline to creditors to Feb. 12

Defaulted Chinese developer Logan Group Co. said that creditors holding over 66% of the group’s $6.2 billion offshore debt have signed an agreement in support of its restructuring plan, a key progress in its yearslong overhaul talk.

The Shenzhen-based homeseller said that other creditors holding over 12% of debt have indicated support, but required more time to complete their internal approval processes due to the Lunar New Year holidays, according to a Hong Kong stock exchange filing late Monday. The early-bird deadline has been extended to Feb. 12, the filing shows.

If Logan is able to obtain such approvals, the company’s support level for the debt plan will surpass 78%, meeting the minimum threshold of 75% required for the execution of a restructuring arrangement in Hong Kong.

That would bring the tussle between Logan, which has more than 150 residential developments in China, and its creditors one step closer toward the finishing line. The builder’s restructuring process has stretched over several years , marked by periods of progress as well as setbacks .

Logan, once the nation’s 20th-biggest builder by sales, updated its restructuring plan earlier this month. The amount of debt eligible for cash payouts to creditors has reduced to a cap of $787 million from $1.3 billion announced in January 2024.

Logan gained support from a group of bondholders on the debt restructuring plan in January, after some negotiation . Prices of the company’s greenback bonds are trading at distressed levels, below 10 cents on the dollar, according to data compiled by Bloomberg.

item.title: Libya’s Ras Lanuf, Es Sider Ports Ordered to Stop Oil Loadings
item.description:
Protesters demanded two of Libya’s major oil ports halt loadings, a move that could hit hundreds of thousands of barrels of exports from the OPEC member.

The stoppages at Ras Lanuf and Es Sider ports start Tuesday, two people with direct knowledge of the situation said. One tanker at Es Sider port has already stopped loading, one person said. Both ports are in the east of the country, where a majority of the crude is produced.

The halts are the latest in Libya’s long-standing struggle to maintain oil production. The industry has been a frequent target in the struggle for dominance in the country that’s been embroiled in turmoil since the overthrow of long-time ruler Moammar Al Qaddafi in 2011. Libya is split between an internationally recognized government in the west and a rival legislature in the east, where military commander Khalifa Haftar holds sway.

On Jan. 5, the so-called Oil Crescent Region Movement, based in the east, threatened to block output and exports if the state-run National Oil Corp. didn’t transfer the headquarters of five energy companies to the region from the west.

Their move is aimed at the Waha, Zueitina, Harouge, Sarir and Mabrouk companies, which operate fields and terminals in the east, and puts at risk about 1 million barrels a day of Libya’s output. The NOC on Jan. 22 put the country’s total daily crude production at 1.43 million barrels.

Besides Ras Lanuf and Es Sider, east Libya has three other oil ports.

item.title: Pork Producer Smithfield’s IPO Raises $522 Million as Price Cut
item.description:
• Shares priced at $20 each, below the marketed range of $23-$27
  • About 26 million shares sold in the initial public offering

Smithfield Foods Inc. ’s initial public offering raised $522 million, with shares pricing below the marketed range.

The world’s largest pork producer and an indirectly owned subsidiary of Hong Kong-listed WH Group Ltd. , sold about 26 million shares for $20 each, according to a statement.

Smithfield and its owner offered 17.4 million shares each for $23 to $27 apiece, a previous filing showed. WH Group, which acquired Smithfield more than a decade ago, will maintain control of the company after the listing.

The offering adds to momentum in the recovering US IPO market. First-time share sales rose to $43 billion last year, 64% higher than 2023 but below the average in the decade prior to the pandemic, data compiled by Bloomberg show.

Well-Known Brands

Smithfield produces packaged meats and fresh pork and sells them under well-known brands including Eckrich, Nathan’s Famous and its eponymous line. It also has a hog production business, which helps supply its fresh pork segment.

The Smithfield, Virginia-based company had net income of $581 million on revenue of $10.2 billion for the nine months ending Sept. 29, compared with a net loss of $2 million on revenue of $10.6 billion a year earlier, according to a filing.

The company has been streamlining its operations ahead of its listing, separating its European operations into a standalone business in August. In December, Smithfield agreed to transfer part of its hog farming operations to a new venture controlled by Murphy Family Ventures LLC . It’s also sold its hog production assets in Utah and some farms in Missouri in recent months, the filing shows.

Its Chinese parent is headed by 84-year-old Chair Wan Long , whose wealth is valued at over $3 billion, according to the Bloomberg Billionaires Index . Born in a village near Luohe in China’s central Henan province, Wan dropped out of high school and joined the army in 1960. His first job was at WH Group’s predecessor, Luohe Cold Storage, in 1968, where he worked his way up to become the factory’s general manager in 1984.

He then turned the factory into Shuanghui Group, which started trading on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange in 1998. The company was renamed WH Group in January 2014 and went public in Hong Kong the same year, making Wan a billionaire. The company acquired Smithfield for $4.7 billion in 2013 in the largest Chinese purchase of a US firm at the time.

President Donald Trump ’s immigration policies, if enacted, will jolt labor-intensive industries like meat-processing. Smithfield warned in its earlier filing that new immigration legislation or increased enforcement efforts could raise its costs or disrupt operations.

Read More: Trump Tells House Republicans He Wants Funds to Finish Wall

Smithfield has been involved in a series of class actions alleging antitrust violations in the pork industry. The company has paid $194 million to settle claims against it, and has been named in similar claims and suits which it intends to defend against, according to the filing.

The offering is being led by Morgan Stanley , Bank of America Corp. and Goldman Sachs Group Inc. , the filing shows. The company expects to list on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol SFD.

item.title: Top Thai Developer Sees Revenue Jump on Chinese Relocations
item.description:
WHA Corp. , Thailand’s largest industrial estate developer, expects revenue to surge at least 39% this year as the company expects US President Donald Trump’s tariff threat on Chinese products to prompt more plant relocations to the Southeast Asian region.

Total revenue this year will exceed 20 billion baht ($590 million), up from an estimated 14.4 billion baht in 2024, Chief Executive Officer Jareeporn Jarukornsakul told a press briefing in Bangkok on Tuesday. WHA expects to post a record profit in 2024, she said, adding full-year earnings are still being audited and will be reported to the stock exchange later.

Thailand’s government is offering a slew of tax and other incentives to attract new foreign direct investment, which have lured companies such as BYD Ltd. , Chongqing Changan Automobile Ltd. and Western Digital Corp. Prime Minister Paetongtarn Shinawatra is banking on new investments to reinvigorate economic growth and employment after near-record household debt levels dented domestic consumption.

“We expect another record year in 2025 as trade tension and the government’s investment promotion policies have significantly attracted foreign direct investments,” said Jareeporn. “WHA needs to accelerate new investments to prepare our land bank and facilities for the expected jump in new customers.”

The company plans to invest 119 billion baht through 2029 to expand industrial properties, warehouses and other digital businesses to meet rising demand, she said. WHA operates 15 industrial complexes in Thailand and Vietnam.

Thailand saw investment applications jump 35% to $33 billion last year, the highest level since 2014, led by foreign direct investment in the digital sector. Amazon.com Inc. ’s web services division and Australia’s NEXTDC Ltd. are among the foreign companies that have announced plans for new data-center investments in Thailand to tap rising demand for AI and cloud-computing services.

WHA’s net income jumped 55% in the first nine months to 3.11 billion baht. Its shares have dropped 11% so far this year in Bangkok, compared with a 4.2% decline in the benchmark SET Index .

item.title: French Bonds Find Buyers as Budget Deal Removes a Fiscal Hurdle
item.description:
• Investors now demand a smaller premium to own French debt
  • More hard choices in store to fix country’s strained finances

Big investment firms from Barings to Jupiter Asset Management are turning positive on French debt , betting Parliament will soon adopt a budget to pull the country back from the brink of near-term fiscal chaos.

With French 10-year bonds yielding around 3.27 %, among the highest in the euro area, buyers are taking the view that, for now, they’re getting paid appropriately for the risks of the political gridlock that left fixed-income investments reeling last year.

“We see value,” said Brian Mangwiro , investment manager in the Barings multi asset team, which has steadily increased exposure. “If we are correct that the budget passes, and that most of the bad news is potentially priced in, French government bonds are quite cheap.”

Mangwiro and others, though, are under no illusions: Getting a 2025 budget through a fragmented Parliament is just the first step in repairing France’s strained finances, so it may only be a short-term reprieve for investors.

France’s political turmoil roiled markets last year, culminating last month when Michel Barnier’s short-lived government collapsed. Government debt delivered a loss of 0.5% to investors in 2024, the only country in the region with a negative return for its sovereign debt, according to a Bloomberg index. Stocks underperformed as well, with the CAC 40 returning less than 1% versus almost 10% for the Stoxx Europe 600 Index.

Barnier’s successor as prime minister, Francois Bayrou , will likely face a no-confidence vote linked to the budget as soon as next week. For now he’s managed to corral enough lawmakers to not vote him out of power, in part by making concessions demanded by the center-left Socialist Party.

Read more: ECB’s Villeroy Says French Budget Plans Go in ‘Right Direction’

That’s helped lure investors. Data from State Street Global Markets on big investors’ securities holdings shows that demand for French bonds is trickling back after aggressive selling last year, though inflows remain well below historical averages.

A €10 billion ($10.5 billion) sale of new bonds last week drew record demand, while the cost of insuring French debt against default has also eased from a five-year peak.

France also stands to gain from further interest-rate cuts at the European Central Bank, almost certainly as soon as this week . That would soothe nerves over the country’s mounting debt costs and provide a tailwind for economic growth. Its debt pile stands at nearly 115% of output, among the highest in Europe.

“We think France is a decent buy now, again,” said Mark Nash , a portfolio manager at Jupiter Asset Management, who had to close a long position as the prospect of a second Trump presidency drove up global yields in the latter part of 2024. “If the ECB can cut and get rates down, then France will be OK.”

Mike Riddell , a portfolio manager at Fidelity International, also owns French government bonds. He pointed to the additional yield that France pays over the region’s safest asset, Germany: That premium at one point surpassed 90 basis points late last year. It’s now around 75 basis points.

‘Reasonable Trade’

“It’s not because I think France is looking wonderful from a macro perspective,” he said. “But France trading at almost one percentage point over bunds to me looks like a reasonable trade.”

Plenty of things can still go wrong. Even if Bayrou manages to pass a budget, further policy decisions are likely to be hamstrung by his narrow base within a fractious Parliament.

On top of that, President Emmanuel Macron can call fresh legislative elections as soon as July, a year after last year’s ballot returned a hung parliament. Should he feel compelled to do that, it would give an opportunity for the far right or far left to have another shot at power.

That would raise the prospect of a government with less investor-friendly policies, or with an even weaker base in the National Assembly than the current one. Further political fragmentation was a risk highlighted by Carmignac bond manager Marie-Anne Allier on Jan. 16, warning that France’s fiscal problems were “absolutely not fixed”.

Given that outlook, RBC BlueBay Asset Management sees an opportunity to go short French bonds versus Germany if the 10-year spread drops below 70 basis points.

Crucially, the alliance between Bayrou and the Socialists may start to crumble if the renegotiation of Macron’s contested 2023 pension law turns sour. The prime minister agreed to revisit the overhaul as part of the concessions to the Socialists, who oppose the ongoing increase in the minimum retirement age.

Halting the reform would be bad news from a fiscal perspective: Bloomberg Economics says doing so could trim 1.2% from GDP by 2050 and lift the debt ratio by almost 15 percentage points above their long-term forecast.

But for now, investors are focused on the imminent progress in passing the budget.

“I think investors are more willing to look at the risks that are on the immediate horizon,” said Barings’ Mangwiro. “Unless there are drastic changes, the impact of pension reforms is likely to be in the distant future.”

item.title: Europe’s IPO Pipeline Expands as Volatility Ebbs, PitchBook Says
item.description:
• Firm sees over 370 European VC-backed firms likely to do IPOs
  • Backlog is weighted toward UK, pharma sector: analyst

Higher valuations and subsiding volatility are laying the groundwork for a significant increase in initial public offerings in Europe this year, according to PitchBook.

The market research firm said that more than 370 European venture capital-backed firms have a high probability of undertaking first-time share sales in 2025, an increase of about 24% since April last year. A recovery in valuations and improved clarity on issues such as interest rates and geopolitics will aid activity, PitchBook said.

“Market-related factors are now more conducive for listings,” Navina Rajan, PitchBook’s analyst for private capital in Europe, Middle East and Africa, said in a Tuesday report. Offerings “are therefore primed to catch up as market conditions are ripe for an open window.”

The findings correspond with optimism that regional IPO activity will gain fresh momentum this year after political turmoil and a list of elections narrowed the windows for offerings in the second half of 2024, following a strong start. Private equity groups are also expected to increasingly join the effort amid mounting pressure to exit investments.

A backlog in IPOs is expanding and weighted toward the UK and pharma sectors, PitchBook said. German and software firms have also significantly grown their list of IPO candidates, it said.

Read More: London Stock Market Shrinks at Fastest Pace in Over a Decade

Among the companies identified by PitchBook with a high likelihood of first-time share sales, count more than 30 with a probability of 90% or more and a combined valuation of roughly €53 billion ($56 billion).

“Whilst we cannot expect all €53 billion to be realized, as many of these candidates will not choose to list, a fraction of this amount would still constitute a significant recovery in exit activity,” Rajan said in the report.

To be sure, the proportion of European companies listing on domestic exchanges fell to a record low of nearly 85% in 2024 as several firms chose the US, PitchBook said. Lagging returns could also weigh on deal activity.

“Whilst better-quality companies should be beneficial for investor returns, this may mean fewer firms come to market,” Rajan said.

item.title: National Bank of Hungary to Hold Key Rate After Inflation Spike
item.description:
Hungary’s central bank is poised keep its main interest rate unchanged for a fourth month after a spike in inflation.

The National Bank of Hungary will keep the benchmark rate at 6.5% on Tuesday, according to all 25 economists in a Bloomberg poll. That would leave it tied with Romania for the highest key rate in the European Union. The decision will be announced at 2 p.m. in Budapest, followed by a statement and a briefing an hour later.

Headline price growth jumped to an annual 4.6% in December, overshooting the central bank’s latest forecast in a “warning sign” to policymakers, Deputy Governor Barnabas Virag told the 24.hu news website last week. He predicted inflation rising close to 5% in January and urged “strong anti-inflationary” policies, including the need to maintain the key rate at current levels, to tackle it.

The forint’s weakening over the past months has fueled inflation, according to Virag. The currency fell 4.6% against the euro in the September-December, though it advanced 0.7% this year, most of it over the past week.

Read more: Hungarian Inflation Jumps, Breaching Central Bank Tolerance Band

The recent forint gains have prompted money market traders to once again start to price in some monetary easing. They now expect about one quarter-point reduction in the key rate in six months. Still, economists at OTP Bank Nyrt., Hungary’s largest lender, said on Jan. 15 that the central bank will probably have no rate-cut room this year due to elevated price growth.

Policymakers target 3% inflation in the medium term, with a 1 percentage point tolerance band around it. Last month, the central bank published its latest projections that forecast reaching the inflation goal sustainably in 2026 versus an earlier estimate for 2025.

item.title: Glencore-Indonesia JV Eyes $1 Billion Sustainability-Linked Loan
item.description:
• DBS, OCBC are arrangers of loan for Aster Chemicals & Energy
  • JV bought Shell’s Singapore refining and chemicals assets

A joint venture between commodity trader Glencore Plc and Indonesia’s PT Chandra Asri Pacific is marketing a $1 billion sustainability-linked syndicated loan, according to people familiar with the matter, as the appeal of ESG-related financing expands in the region.

DBS Bank Ltd. and Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp. are the arrangers of the facility, which has an average life of 6.3 years, the people said, who asked not to be named discussing private matters. Aster Chemicals & Energy, a new entity under the joint venture called CAPGC, is the borrower, the people added.

CAPGC, which is purchasing Shell ’s Singapore-based refinery business, is majority-owned and operated by Chandra Asri, while Glencore owns a minority stake through subsidiary companies, according to the Indonesian firm’s website .

Glencore declined to comment, while Chandra Asri didn’t respond to a request for comment.

Asia Pacific companies are increasingly incorporating environmental, social and governance-linked elements into their financing as they look to boost decarbonization efforts. Borrowers in Asia Pacific outside Japan raised $66.5 billion of sustainability-linked loans in 2024, a 37% jump from the year prior, according to Bloomberg-compiled data.

The loan for Aster Chemicals & Energy pays an interest margin of 188 basis points over the risk-free Secured Overnight Financing Rate, the people said, adding that proceeds are for general corporate purposes.

Last May, CAPGC agreed to purchase Shell Singapore Pte.’s interest in Shell Energy & Chemicals Park Singapore, which comprises refining and chemical assets on Bukom and Jurong islands.
